title: SEGMENT 10 — The Heidelberg screen: Nüsslein-Volhard, Wieschaus, and the day systematic genetics was born

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — The Heidelberg screen: Nüsslein-Volhard, Wieschaus, and the day systematic genetics was born
Welcome back.
We left off with the fly as a fully modern molecular platform.

Now I want to take you back to 1979, to a lab in Heidelberg, Germany, where two scientists named Christiane Nüsslein-Volhard and Eric Wieschaus were about to do an experiment that, when it was published in 1980, would rewrite developmental biology.
The question they asked was simple and audacious.
Of all the genes in the fly genome, which ones are required for laying out the embryonic body plan?
Which genes specify the head, the thorax, the abdomen?
Which ones determine that each segment looks different from its neighbors?
Which ones tell the embryo which end is front and which is back?
They didn't want to identify just a few key genes.
They wanted to identify all of them — the complete genetic toolkit for embryonic patterning.
Every gene whose loss disrupted the basic body plan, they wanted to catalogue.
To do this, they ran what is now called the Heidelberg screen — and what was, at the time, the most ambitious systematic mutagenesis screen ever attempted in any animal.
They mutagenized tens of thousands of male flies with the chemical EMS.
They crossed those males through a defined breeding scheme that segregated mutations to homozygosity over three generations.
They looked at the resulting embryos under a microscope.
Hundreds of thousands of embryos.
Every embryo, scored for defects in body plan: missing segments, fused segments, mirror-image duplications, polarity reversals, anything weird.
The analogy: the Heidelberg screen is the day systematic genetics was born.

Nüsslein-Volhard and Wieschaus mutagenized hundreds of thousands of flies, scored every embryonic pattern defect, found the maternal-effect and segmentation genes — and in doing so rewrote developmental biology from the bottom up.

It was the first proof that you could saturate a genetic system — find essentially every gene required for a particular biological process — by sheer brute-force screening, if you picked the right organism and asked the right question.
The screen identified about 120 genes whose mutants had embryonic patterning defects.
The genes fell into beautifully clean categories:
Maternal-effect genes — genes whose function in the mother sets up the embryo's basic axes.
bicoid specifies the anterior end.
nanos specifies the posterior end.
Embryos from bicoid-mutant mothers have two posterior ends and no head.
Embryos from nanos-mutant mothers have two anterior ends and no abdomen.
These genes encode mRNAs that the mother deposits in specific locations in the egg, where they translate into protein gradients that pattern the body axis.
The first complete molecular model of an axis-setting morphogen — Bicoid — came out of this screen.
Gap genes — genes whose mutants are missing big chunks of the body.
Krüppel, hunchback, knirps, giant.
These genes are turned on in broad bands across the embryo by the maternal gradients, and they themselves activate the next layer of patterning.
Pair-rule genes — genes whose mutants are missing every other segment.
even-skipped, fushi tarazu, hairy, runt.
These are expressed in seven stripes along the body, each stripe corresponding to a pair of future segments.
Segment-polarity genes — genes whose mutants have polarity defects within each segment.
engrailed, wingless, hedgehog.
These define the front-versus-back orientation within each segment.

What's astonishing about this hierarchy is not just that Nüsslein-Volhard and Wieschaus found all these classes — it's that the molecular biology, worked out over the following two decades, matched the genetic logic exactly.
The maternal gradients turn on the gap genes.
The gap genes, in overlapping combinations, turn on the pair-rule genes in their seven-stripe patterns.
The pair-rule genes turn on the segment-polarity genes within each segment.
The hierarchy you can infer from the mutant phenotypes is the hierarchy of the actual molecular cascade.
The genetic screen, run in 1979, predicted the molecular logic that would be discovered piece by piece through the 1980s and 1990s.
And — here's the punchline that should be familiar by now — almost every one of these genes has a vertebrate homolog doing roughly the same job.
Hedgehog signaling in flies turned out to be Sonic Hedgehog in vertebrates, controlling limb patterning, neural tube specification, and a host of other developmental processes.
Wingless in flies is Wnt in vertebrates, one of the major signaling pathways in development and cancer.
Engrailed, hunchback, even-skipped — all have vertebrate counterparts.
The molecular grammar of embryonic patterning, worked out in fly, generalizes to vertebrates.
Mutations in the human homologs of these genes cause major birth defects.
Nüsslein-Volhard and Wieschaus shared the Nobel Prize in 1995 with Edward Lewis, who had done parallel pioneering work on homeotic genes.
The Heidelberg screen is the textbook example of a saturation screen: a screen ambitious enough that it identifies essentially every gene required for a given biological process.
Almost every subsequent forward genetic screen in any organism — including, as we'll see, the Boston-Tübingen zebrafish screen in 1996 — was modeled on the Heidelberg approach.
The big idea: systematic mutagenesis, on a sufficiently large scale, can identify the complete genetic toolkit for a biological process.
The fly was the first animal in which this was demonstrated.
The screen logic generalizes; the patterning genes generalize; the strategy is now standard.
Heidelberg was the proof of concept.
